lessons.title: The Cell Cycle and Mitosis
objectives: Describe the phases of the cell cycle: interphase (G1, S, G2) and M phase
Explain the purpose of DNA replication in the S phase
Describe the stages of mitosis and their outcomes
Explain the clinical relevance of uncontrolled cell division (cancer)

conceptBlocks.heading: The Cell Cycle
conceptBlocks.body:
The cell cycle is the sequence of events a cell passes through from formation to division:

**Interphase** (90% of cycle time):
• **G1 phase** — cell grows, synthesizes proteins and organelles; prepares for DNA replication
• **S phase** — DNA is replicated (each chromosome is duplicated to form two identical sister chromatids)
• **G2 phase** — cell continues growing; checks DNA replication accuracy; prepares for division

**M phase** — mitosis + cytokinesis (actual cell division)

**G0 phase** — non-dividing state; many mature specialized cells (neurons, cardiac muscle) remain permanently in G0.

conceptBlocks.heading: Mitosis: Producing Identical Daughter Cells
conceptBlocks.body:
Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells, each with the same chromosome number as the parent (diploid, 46 chromosomes in humans).

Stages of mitosis (PMAT — Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase):

• **Prophase** — chromosomes condense and become visible; spindle fibers form
• **Metaphase** — chromosomes align at the center (metaphase plate); spindle fibers attach to each chromatid
• **Anaphase** — sister chromatids are pulled apart to opposite poles of the cell
• **Telophase + Cytokinesis** — nuclear envelopes re-form; cell pinches in two → two identical daughter cells

Mitosis is used for: growth, tissue repair, and replacement of short-lived cells (RBCs, skin cells, GI lining).

conceptBlocks.heading: Cancer: Uncontrolled Mitosis
conceptBlocks.body:
Cell cycle progression is controlled by checkpoints:
• G1 checkpoint — is the cell large enough? Is DNA undamaged?
• G2 checkpoint — was DNA replicated accurately?
• Spindle checkpoint (M) — are chromosomes properly attached?

**Proto-oncogenes** — normal genes that promote cell division. When mutated → **oncogenes** → drive uncontrolled division.
**Tumor suppressor genes** (e.g., p53, BRCA1) — normally apply the brakes. When mutated/deleted → no brake → cancer.

Many chemotherapy drugs target rapidly dividing cells at specific cell cycle phases (e.g., taxanes block the spindle checkpoint in M phase; antimetabolites block DNA replication in S phase). This explains why chemo also affects rapidly dividing normal cells (hair follicles, GI mucosa, bone marrow).

lessons.teachingNotes: PMAT mnemonic is standard. Focus clinical teaching on the cancer connection — why chemo causes hair loss and bone marrow suppression. Trace: chemo targets any rapidly dividing cell → cancer cells + hair follicle cells + GI cells + bone marrow progenitors → side effects of nausea, alopecia, and neutropenia.

lessons.title: Meiosis and Clinical Relevance
objectives: Distinguish meiosis from mitosis in purpose, process, and outcome
Explain how meiosis produces haploid gametes
Describe nondisjunction and its consequences (e.g., Down syndrome, Turner syndrome)
Identify the clinical relevance of meiotic errors in reproductive health

conceptBlocks.heading: Meiosis: Producing Gametes
conceptBlocks.body:
Meiosis occurs only in gonads (ovaries and testes) to produce sex cells (gametes: egg and sperm).

Key differences from mitosis:
• **Two rounds of division** (Meiosis I and Meiosis II) from one parent cell
• **Four daughter cells** produced (vs two in mitosis)
• **Haploid outcome** — each daughter cell has 23 chromosomes (vs 46 in mitosis)
• **Genetic recombination** — crossing over during Prophase I shuffles genetic material, creating unique combinations

**Why haploid?** When sperm (23 chromosomes) fertilizes egg (23 chromosomes) → zygote (46 chromosomes). If gametes were diploid, each generation would double the chromosome count.

conceptBlocks.heading: Nondisjunction: Chromosomal Errors
conceptBlocks.body:
**Nondisjunction** = failure of chromosomes to separate properly during meiosis I or II → daughter cells receive too many or too few chromosomes.

Consequences:

**Trisomy 21 (Down syndrome)** — extra chromosome 21 (47 chromosomes total); caused by nondisjunction in meiosis I of egg formation, more common with advanced maternal age

**Turner syndrome (45, X)** — missing one X chromosome in females; nondisjunction produces an egg or sperm with no sex chromosome

**Klinefelter syndrome (47, XXY)** — males with an extra X; usually nondisjunction of X chromosomes in egg formation

Risk of nondisjunction increases with maternal age — basis for genetic counseling and prenatal screening (amniocentesis, chorionic villus sampling).

conceptBlocks.heading: Mitosis vs Meiosis: Quick Comparison
conceptBlocks.body:
| Feature | Mitosis | Meiosis |
|---|---|---|
| Location | All dividing body cells | Gonads (ovaries, testes) |
| Purpose | Growth, repair, replacement | Gamete production |
| Divisions | 1 | 2 |
| Daughter cells | 2 | 4 |
| Chromosome # | 46 (diploid) | 23 (haploid) |
| Genetic outcome | Identical to parent | Genetically unique |

lessons.teachingNotes: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) is a universally familiar example that makes nondisjunction concrete and human. Include that maternal age is the primary risk factor — the basis for recommending prenatal genetic screening for mothers over 35. Connect nondisjunction to clinical practice: genetic counselors explain these risks, and nurses must understand the basics to support patients receiving these conversations.

questions.stem: During which phase of the cell cycle is DNA replicated?

options.A: G1 phase
options.B: S phase (synthesis phase)
options.C: G2 phase
options.D: M phase (mitosis)

questions.rationale: DNA replication occurs during the S (synthesis) phase of interphase. The G1 phase prepares the cell for replication (growth, protein synthesis). The G2 phase follows replication and prepares for division. The M phase (mitosis) is when the replicated chromosomes are separated and the cell physically divides.
questions.keyConceptTested: S phase = DNA replication

questions.stem: Mitosis produces:

options.A: Four haploid daughter cells genetically unique from the parent
options.B: Two diploid daughter cells genetically identical to the parent
options.C: Two haploid daughter cells for sexual reproduction
options.D: Four diploid daughter cells for tissue repair

questions.rationale: Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells, each with the full diploid complement of chromosomes (46 in humans). This is used for growth, tissue repair, and cell replacement. Meiosis (not mitosis) produces four haploid (23 chromosomes) genetically unique daughter cells for sexual reproduction.
questions.keyConceptTested: mitosis outcome — 2 diploid identical daughters

questions.stem: Why must gametes (eggs and sperm) be haploid (23 chromosomes) rather than diploid?

options.A: Haploid cells are smaller and can move more quickly through reproductive tracts
options.B: Fertilization combines two gametes, so each must contribute half the chromosome complement to produce a normal diploid zygote
options.C: Haploid cells do not require energy to divide, making reproduction more efficient
options.D: Haploid gametes contain only maternal or paternal genes, ensuring genetic purity

questions.rationale: If gametes were diploid (46 chromosomes), fertilization would produce an offspring with 92 chromosomes — incompatible with normal development. Meiosis halves the chromosome number to 23 (haploid), so when sperm (23) + egg (23) unite, the zygote has the correct 46 chromosomes (diploid). This mechanism maintains chromosomal stability across generations.
questions.keyConceptTested: reason for meiosis producing haploid cells

questions.stem: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) results from:

options.A: A mutation in a single gene on chromosome 21
options.B: Deletion of part of chromosome 21
options.C: Nondisjunction during meiosis, resulting in an egg or sperm with an extra copy of chromosome 21
options.D: A viral infection that inserts extra DNA into chromosome 21 during fetal development

questions.rationale: Trisomy 21 most commonly results from nondisjunction — failure of chromosome 21 pairs to separate properly during meiosis I. The resulting gamete has 24 chromosomes instead of 23. When fertilized, the zygote has 47 chromosomes (three copies of chromosome 21). Risk increases significantly with advanced maternal age.
questions.keyConceptTested: nondisjunction → trisomy 21 (Down syndrome)

questions.stem: During which stage of mitosis do chromosomes align at the center (metaphase plate) of the cell?

options.A: Prophase
options.B: Metaphase
options.C: Anaphase
options.D: Telophase

questions.rationale: During metaphase, chromosomes align at the metaphase plate (center of the cell) and spindle fibers attach to each sister chromatid. Prophase = chromosomes condense. Anaphase = sister chromatids pulled apart. Telophase = nuclear envelopes re-form and the cell divides. PMAT helps recall the order.
questions.keyConceptTested: metaphase — chromosome alignment

questions.stem: Chemotherapy drugs that target the spindle checkpoint (e.g., taxanes) cause side effects in hair follicles and bone marrow because:

options.A: These drugs accumulate in hair and bone marrow due to poor liver metabolism
options.B: Hair follicle and bone marrow cells are rapidly dividing, making them susceptible to cell cycle inhibition alongside cancer cells
options.C: These drugs specifically target keratin proteins found in hair and bone marrow
options.D: Taxanes block the G0 phase, forcing hair and bone marrow cells to divide uncontrollably

questions.rationale: Many chemotherapy agents target rapidly dividing cells. Cancer cells are rapidly dividing — but so are normal hair follicle cells, GI mucosal cells, and bone marrow progenitor cells. Spindle inhibitors (e.g., taxanes) disrupt the mitotic spindle in ANY rapidly dividing cell → hair loss (alopecia), mucositis, and bone marrow suppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia, anemia) as collateral effects.
questions.keyConceptTested: chemotherapy side effects — targeting rapidly dividing cells

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the purpose of the G2 checkpoint in the cell cycle?

options.A: To verify that the cell has enough nutrients to proceed with protein synthesis
options.B: To ensure that DNA replication was completed accurately before the cell enters mitosis
options.C: To confirm that gametes have been successfully formed
options.D: To initiate the G0 non-dividing phase in differentiated cells

questions.rationale: The G2 checkpoint verifies that DNA replication (S phase) was completed accurately and that any DNA damage has been repaired before the cell proceeds into mitosis. If errors are detected, cell cycle arrest occurs to allow repair or trigger apoptosis. This checkpoint prevents cells with damaged DNA from dividing — a key cancer prevention mechanism.
questions.keyConceptTested: G2 checkpoint — DNA accuracy check before mitosis

questions.stem: Turner syndrome (45, X) occurs in females with only one sex chromosome. This chromosomal abnormality most commonly results from:

options.A: A mutation in a gene controlling sex hormone production
options.B: Nondisjunction producing a gamete with no sex chromosome
options.C: Deletion of a gene on the X chromosome
options.D: Trisomy of the X chromosome

questions.rationale: Turner syndrome (karyotype 45, X) occurs when an egg or sperm fails to contribute a sex chromosome due to nondisjunction. The resulting individual has only 45 total chromosomes (one X, no second sex chromosome). Features include short stature, gonadal dysgenesis, and infertility. This is distinct from a gene mutation or deletion.
questions.keyConceptTested: Turner syndrome — nondisjunction; monosomy X

questions.stem: How does genetic recombination (crossing over) during Prophase I of meiosis contribute to genetic diversity?

options.A: It doubles the number of chromosomes in each gamete
options.B: It deletes harmful genes from the germline
options.C: It exchanges segments of DNA between homologous chromosomes, creating new combinations of alleles
options.D: It produces identical copies of chromosomes to ensure consistent genetic transmission

questions.rationale: During Prophase I, homologous chromosomes pair up and exchange segments of DNA (crossing over). This shuffles alleles between chromosomes, producing genetically unique combinations not present in either parent's original chromosomes. This is the primary source of genetic variation in sexually reproducing organisms — each gamete is genetically unique.
questions.keyConceptTested: crossing over — genetic recombination during meiosis I

questions.stem: Neurons in the adult brain are generally considered to be in the G0 phase. This means they:

options.A: Divide very rapidly to replace damaged neurons
options.B: Are in a non-dividing state and have limited regenerative capacity after injury
options.C: Undergo meiosis to produce neural stem cells
options.D: Continuously replicate their DNA without dividing

questions.rationale: Most adult neurons are in G0 — a non-dividing, terminally differentiated state. This is why brain injury (stroke, trauma) causes permanent neurological deficits — lost neurons are generally not replaced. This contrasts with rapidly dividing cells (skin, GI lining, bone marrow) which can regenerate. It is also why neurodegenerative diseases are difficult to treat — neurons lost to Alzheimer's or Parkinson's disease cannot be replaced by cell division.
questions.keyConceptTested: G0 phase — non-dividing neurons; clinical implication

questions.stem: A tumor suppressor gene such as p53 normally functions to:

options.A: Promote rapid cell division to maintain organ size
options.B: Stimulate oncogene expression when growth is needed
options.C: Stop the cell cycle when DNA damage is detected, allowing repair or triggering apoptosis
options.D: Produce structural proteins for the mitotic spindle

questions.rationale: Tumor suppressor genes (e.g., p53, BRCA1) act as brakes on the cell cycle. p53, activated by DNA damage, halts the cell cycle at the G1 checkpoint to allow DNA repair. If repair is impossible, p53 triggers apoptosis (programmed cell death). Loss or mutation of both copies of a tumor suppressor gene removes these brakes, enabling uncontrolled cell division (cancer).
questions.keyConceptTested: tumor suppressor gene function — p53

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly differentiates meiosis from mitosis?

options.A: Meiosis produces 2 diploid cells; mitosis produces 4 haploid cells
options.B: Meiosis involves 2 rounds of division producing 4 haploid cells; mitosis involves 1 division producing 2 diploid cells
options.C: Both mitosis and meiosis produce genetically identical daughter cells
options.D: Mitosis occurs only in the gonads; meiosis occurs in all dividing body cells

questions.rationale: Meiosis = 2 consecutive divisions → 4 haploid (23 chromosome) daughter cells that are genetically unique (crossing over). Mitosis = 1 division → 2 diploid (46 chromosome) daughter cells genetically identical to the parent. Mitosis occurs throughout the body; meiosis occurs only in gonads.
questions.keyConceptTested: mitosis vs meiosis — complete comparison

questions.stem: During prophase of mitosis, which two visible changes occur in the cell?

options.A: Chromosomes align at the metaphase plate; spindle fibers attach
options.B: Chromosomes condense into visible structures; the mitotic spindle begins to form
options.C: Sister chromatids separate and move to opposite poles
options.D: Nuclear envelopes re-form around each set of chromosomes

questions.rationale: In prophase: (1) chromatin condenses into distinct visible chromosomes (each already a pair of sister chromatids joined at the centromere), and (2) the mitotic spindle begins assembling from microtubules radiating from the centrosomes. The nuclear envelope breaks down late in prophase (prometaphase). Chromosome alignment = metaphase. Chromatid separation = anaphase. Nuclear envelope re-formation = telophase.
questions.keyConceptTested: prophase — chromosome condensation + spindle formation

questions.stem: During anaphase of mitosis, spindle fibers shorten and pull sister chromatids to opposite poles. What does each pole of the dividing cell receive?

options.A: A complete set of 92 chromatids (double the normal)
options.B: A complete set of 46 chromosomes (now single-stranded, as sisters have separated)
options.C: 23 chromosomes (the haploid number for gamete production)
options.D: Random assortments of chromosomes with no guaranteed complete set

questions.rationale: In anaphase, cohesins holding sister chromatids together are cleaved by separase. Spindle fibers shorten, pulling one chromatid from each pair to each pole. Each pole receives one copy of every chromosome — a full complement of 46 single chromatids (now called chromosomes). This ensures each daughter cell receives a genetically identical diploid set. The 23-chromosome haploid number occurs in meiosis only.
questions.keyConceptTested: anaphase — sister chromatid separation; each pole receives 46

questions.stem: Telophase and cytokinesis complete cell division. What is the final outcome of these two processes?

options.A: Two cells, each with 92 chromosomes (one copy of each chromosome pair)
options.B: Four haploid cells genetically identical to the parent
options.C: Two genetically identical diploid cells, each with a distinct nucleus and separated cytoplasm
options.D: One cell with a double nucleus awaiting the next round of division

questions.rationale: In telophase, nuclear envelopes re-form around each set of chromosomes at the poles, and chromosomes begin to decondense. Cytokinesis (cleavage furrow in animal cells) then pinches the cytoplasm into two separate cells. The result is two genetically identical daughter cells, each diploid (46 chromosomes), ready to enter G1 of a new cell cycle.
questions.keyConceptTested: telophase + cytokinesis — two identical diploid daughter cells

questions.stem: Klinefelter syndrome (47, XXY) most commonly results from:

options.A: A mutation in a gene controlling male sexual development on the Y chromosome
options.B: Nondisjunction of sex chromosomes during meiosis, producing a gamete with both X and Y
options.C: Deletion of part of the X chromosome
options.D: Trisomy of chromosome 21 rather than sex chromosomes

questions.rationale: Klinefelter syndrome (47, XXY) occurs when nondisjunction produces a gamete containing both X and Y chromosomes (from the father — XY sperm) or two X chromosomes (from the mother — XX egg). Fertilization then results in a 47, XXY zygote. Features include tall stature, small testes, infertility, and gynecomastia. It is the most common sex chromosome aneuploidy in males (1 in 500–1000).
questions.keyConceptTested: Klinefelter syndrome — 47,XXY; nondisjunction

questions.stem: Proto-oncogenes are normal genes that regulate cell growth and division. They become dangerous when they mutate into oncogenes because:

options.A: They produce tumor suppressor proteins that prevent normal DNA repair
options.B: Mutated oncogenes drive continuous, unregulated cell division even without growth signals
options.C: They cause cells to enter G0 permanently, preventing normal tissue renewal
options.D: Oncogenes suppress the immune system, allowing cancer cells to evade detection

questions.rationale: Proto-oncogenes encode proteins that normally promote controlled cell cycle progression (growth factors, receptors, signaling molecules). A gain-of-function mutation converts them into oncogenes — which produce continuously active (always 'on') growth signals, driving uncontrolled cell proliferation. RAS is a classic example: mutant RAS (KRAS) continuously signals cell division, found in ~30% of human cancers.
questions.keyConceptTested: oncogenes — gain-of-function; continuous growth signaling

questions.stem: The G1 checkpoint in the cell cycle evaluates which of the following before allowing the cell to proceed to S phase?

options.A: Whether the mitotic spindle is properly assembled
options.B: Whether all chromosomes are attached to spindle fibers
options.C: Whether the cell is large enough and its DNA is undamaged, and whether growth signals are present
options.D: Whether DNA replication has been completed accurately

questions.rationale: The G1 checkpoint (restriction point) evaluates: (1) cell size and nutrient availability, (2) DNA integrity (absence of double-strand breaks), and (3) presence of mitogenic (growth-promoting) signals. If these conditions are met, the cell proceeds to S phase. If DNA is damaged, p53 halts the cycle here. This is the major decision point for whether a cell will divide. The G2 checkpoint checks DNA replication accuracy; the spindle checkpoint verifies chromosome attachment.
questions.keyConceptTested: G1 checkpoint — cell size, DNA integrity, growth signals

questions.stem: The key difference between Meiosis I and Meiosis II is that:

options.A: Meiosis I separates sister chromatids; Meiosis II separates homologous chromosome pairs
options.B: Meiosis I separates homologous chromosome pairs (reducing chromosome number); Meiosis II separates sister chromatids
options.C: Both divisions separate the same structures, but Meiosis II is the first to produce haploid cells
options.D: Meiosis I only occurs in females; Meiosis II only in males

questions.rationale: Meiosis I is the reductional division: homologous chromosome pairs (each consisting of two sister chromatids) separate → daughter cells receive 23 chromosomes (each still as two chromatids). This is where genetic recombination (crossing over) also occurs. Meiosis II is the equational division: sister chromatids separate (like mitosis), producing 4 haploid cells total with 23 single chromosomes each.
questions.keyConceptTested: meiosis I vs II — reductional vs equational division

questions.stem: Colchicine, derived from the autumn crocus plant, arrests cells in metaphase by disrupting microtubule polymerization. This drug has been used clinically to:

options.A: Treat bacterial infections by targeting prokaryotic cell division
options.B: Treat gout by preventing neutrophil migration (also microtubule-dependent) and reduce inflammation
options.C: Stimulate bone marrow cell division to treat anemia
options.D: Block meiosis in reproductive cells to prevent genetic disease

questions.rationale: Colchicine is used clinically to treat acute gout attacks. It disrupts microtubule polymerization, which impairs neutrophil motility and activation — reducing the inflammatory response in gouty joints. It is also used in laboratory karyotyping to arrest cells in metaphase for chromosome analysis. Colchicine does not have antibiotic, hematopoietic, or reproductive applications.
questions.keyConceptTested: colchicine — microtubule disruption; gout treatment; karyotyping
